elements.sent-2411-001: Tactile interaction plays an essential role in human-to-human interaction.
elements.sent-2411-002: People gain comfort and support from tactile interactions with others and touch is an important predictor for trust.
elements.sent-2411-003: While touch has been explored as a communicative modality in HCI and HRI, we here report on two studies in which touching a social robot is used to regulate people's stress levels and consequently their actions.
elements.sent-2411-004: In the first study, we look at whether different intensities of tactile interaction result in a physiological response related to stress, and whether the interaction impacts risk-taking behaviour and trust.
elements.sent-2411-005: We let 38 participants complete a Balloon Analogue Risk Task (BART), a computer-based game that serves as a proxy for risk-taking behaviour.
elements.sent-2411-006: In our study, participants are supported by a robot during the BART task.
elements.sent-2411-007: The robot builds trust and encourages participants to take more risk.
elements.sent-2411-008: The results show that affective tactile interaction with the robot increases participants' risk-taking behaviour, but gentle affective tactile interaction increases comfort and lowers stress whereas high-intensity touch does not.
elements.sent-2411-009: We also find that male participants exhibit more risk-taking behaviour than females while being less stressed.
elements.sent-2411-010: Based on this experiment, a second study is used to ascertain whether these effects are caused by the social nature of tactile interaction or by the physical interaction alone.
elements.sent-2411-011: For this, instead of a social robot, participants now have a tactile interaction with a non-social device.
elements.sent-2411-012: The non-social interaction does not result in any effect, leading us to conclude that tactile interaction with humanoid robots is a social phenomenon rather than a mere physical phenomenon.
elements.sent-2411-013: The application of haptic and tactile interaction is expected to see significant growth in human-robot interaction (HRI) research.
elements.sent-2411-014: Haptic interaction, in this context a physical interaction with digital media \cite{lee2006physically , conveys emotions and promotes a sense of interpersonal closeness \cite{andreasson2018affective .}}
elements.sent-2411-015: As robots enter social spaces, they will have the opportunity to join in similar physical interactions.
elements.sent-2411-016: And as tactile interaction has been found to support communication and strengthen connections between individuals, often contributing to overall well-being \cite{zhou2021tactile,chan2020effect , we expect similar effects to manifest in people when engaging in tactile interactions with robots.}
elements.subsection-2411-001: Physical interaction between people and robots
elements.sent-2411-017: People often bond with friends and team members by shaking hands, giving high fives, or bumping fists.
elements.sent-2411-018: As robots start to become a part of our social lives, they are likely to take part in these kinds of physical greetings and interactions \cite{fitter2016equipping .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Physical interaction with robots is relatively uncommon for several reasons.
elements.sent-2411-020: One significant issue is the lack of safety during haptic interactions \cite{li2017touching .}
elements.sent-2411-021: Simple greetings like hugging, holding, or shaking hands already form a safety concern.
elements.sent-2411-022: Although many robots are explicitly designed for human-robot interaction (HRI), tactile or haptic interaction has in many cases been an afterthought.
elements.sent-2411-023: For example, the two best-selling social robots, the United Robotics Group's Nao and Pepper robots, both have pinch points that can trap fingers making these robots unsuitable for tactile interaction.
elements.sent-2411-024: Only a limited number of robots are explicitly designed to allow physical human-robot interaction, with most other robots prone to suffer breakage when being manipulated or handled with forces that would not be uncommon in interactions between people \cite{alami2006safe .}
elements.sent-2411-025: There is a significant body of literature that reports on the impact of physical and haptic interaction between people on attitudes, emotions, and performance.
elements.sent-2411-026: For example, even subtle physical contact between individuals can enhance feelings of security and increase risk-taking behaviours \cite{hri1 .}
elements.sent-2411-027: Similarly, haptic and physical interaction has been shown to alter physiological responses in human-robot interaction.
elements.sent-2411-028: The current study aims to investigate the impact of the intensity of tactile interaction on people's attitudes, stress levels, arousal, and task performance with a robot, with a specific focus on understanding gender differences in physical and physiological responses during a task.
elements.sent-2411-029: Ultimately, the aim is to contribute to an understanding of the potential benefits of human-robot tactile interaction for physical and physiological well-being.
elements.sent-2411-030: Affective touch has been shown to reduce feelings of stress and anxiety.
elements.sent-2411-031: Affective touch increases dopamine and serotonin levels, leading to improvements in mood and reductions in discomfort \cite{von2017soothing .}
elements.sent-2411-032: Recent research has also suggested that affective touch can be an effective means of reducing social exclusion, with studies finding that participants are more willing to participate in experimental tasks when touching is perceived to be in line with their goals \cite{sailer2022meaning .}
elements.sent-2411-033: Affective touch also has the potential to reduce social pain associated with exclusion \cite{von2017soothing .}
elements.sent-2411-034: Studies have found that slow, emotive touch influences the perception of physical pain and appears to be mediated by a distinct tactile neurophysiological system \cite{hri4 .}
elements.sent-2411-035: It is believed that the interpretation of affective touch is determined by the perceived meaning of the touch, which is influenced by the match between the touch's purpose and the receiver's goals \cite{jakubiak2016keep .}
elements.sent-2411-036: The literature suggests that social presence, such as human tactile interaction, can increase human trust and touch may be instrumental for building trust \cite{walter2013trust .}
elements.sent-2411-037: While touch between people who are unfamiliar with each other is often short, under certain circumstances prolonged touch between parties that are not intimate can be comfortable, with massage being a prime example here \cite{sailer2022meaning .}
elements.sent-2411-038: Next to being merely relaxing, touch also conveys emotion and meaning in a larger social context \cite{jakubiak2016keep .}
elements.sent-2411-039: There is a small but significant body of literature that describes the use of robots to support tactile communication with people, and research has shown that touch can influence emotions, stress levels, and physiological arousal \cite{ren2023behavioural, ren2022tactile .}
elements.sent-2411-040: For instance, it was found that tactile interactions with a PARO robot can decrease pain perception and elevate salivary oxytocin levels \cite{hri5 .}
elements.sent-2411-041: Li et al. found that touching a humanoid robot with identifiable body parts leads to significant changes in arousal levels, providing evidence that tactile HRI elicits physiological responses \cite{li2017touching .}
elements.sent-2411-042: Chan et al. demonstrated that remotely delivered affective touch via a robot leads to changes in psychological experience and skin conductance response (SCR) \cite{chan2020effect .}
elements.sent-2411-043: This paper focuses on the impact of physical and haptic interaction on decision taking, and specifically on risk-taking behaviour.
elements.sent-2411-044: Risk-taking is a fundamental human activity with important implications for individuals' health and social lives.
elements.sent-2411-045: One significant factor that has been shown to influence risk-taking behaviour is peer pressure \cite{sandstrom2011power .}
elements.sent-2411-046: When individuals observe others, particularly friends or peers, engaging in risky behaviour and seeking validation, they are more likely to participate in those behaviours themselves.
elements.sent-2411-047: This is evident in various situations, such as in drivers who engage in reckless driving when encouraged by others, leading to an increased risk of accidents \cite{hri9, hri10 .}
elements.sent-2411-048: As risk-taking behaviour is mediated by the presence and encouragement of others, we set out to study whether similar effects can be observed in HRI.
elements.sent-2411-049: This study aims to investigate whether a robot has a similar impact on risk-taking behaviour as peer influence does in human-human interactions, and to understand the impact of physical and haptic interaction on risk-taking.
elements.sent-2411-050: Previous research already confirmed that a robot's verbal encouragement can influence human risk-taking behaviour in participants \cite{hri2,kennedy2014children,vollmer2018children, salomons2018humans .}
elements.sent-2411-051: In the studies reported here, we focus on the impact of affective touch and touch intensity on risk-taking behaviour, by measuring physiological arousal levels through skin conductivity (SC) and heart rate variability (HRV).
elements.sent-2411-052: Additionally, we investigate the effect of affective touch on trust .
elements.sent-2411-053: Overall, our study aims to contribute to our understanding of the impact of affective touch in HRI on the user's physiological response and behaviour.
elements.sent-2411-054: This is to an extent related to trust.
elements.sent-2411-055: Trust is an increasingly important aspect of human-robot interactions (HRI) as interactions become more complex and have more consequential outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-056: Previous research has shown that social presence, including human tactile interaction, can enhance trust between humans and robots\cite{wang2019influence,law2021touching .}
elements.sent-2411-057: The social nature of tactile interaction refers to the interpersonal and communicative aspects associated with physical touch between individuals.
elements.sent-2411-058: It encompasses the way touch is used to convey emotions, intentions, or establish social connections.
elements.sent-2411-059: Touch serves as our primary non-verbal means of expressing intimate emotions and is crucial for our physical and emotional well-being \cite{van2015social .}
elements.sent-2411-060: In today's digital era, much of our social interaction occurs through technology, but current communication tools like video calls lack the ability to convey touch sensations.
elements.sent-2411-061: This limitation means that digital communication cannot replicate the deep emotional connections formed through physical touch.
elements.sent-2411-062: To incorporate social touch into technology, systems need to not only generate touch sensations but also interpret \cite{ren2023low and respond to touch.}
elements.sent-2411-063: Social touch can have various effects on the receiver, including physiological, psychological, behavioural, and social impacts, and these effects often interact with one another.
elements.sent-2411-064: For example, a social presence and emotions can mutually reinforce each other during social touch interactions \cite{van2015social .}
elements.sent-2411-065: In the second study, we sought to determine whether the observed effects of the first study are primarily influenced by the social nature of tactile interaction or by the physical interaction itself.
elements.sent-2411-066: The following section describes the human-robot interaction experimental design, including the research materials, experimental procedures, and data collection procedures.
elements.sent-2411-067: The data processing methodology and performance metrics used in the study are introduced in Section~\ref{sec3:results_analysis .}
elements.sent-2411-068: To ascertain the social nature of touching a robot, we replicate the human-robot interaction study, but instead with a non-social object in Section~\ref{sec4:hoi_ex .}
elements.sent-2411-069: Results are presented together with a discussion in Section~\ref{sec:discussion , along with reflections on the limitations of our research.}
elements.sent-2411-070: The goal of the present study is to investigate the impact of tactile interaction on the physiological stress response, on risk-taking behaviour and on trust in the context of HRI.
elements.sent-2411-072: The first study examines the effects of different intensities of tactile interaction on stress responses and risk-taking behaviour.
elements.sent-2411-073: The second study determines whether the effects observed in the first study are caused by the social nature of the tactile interaction or by the physical interaction alone.
elements.sent-2411-074: Our research answers the following research questions:.

elements.sent-2411-084: Participants can earn more by inflating the balloon, but risk losing their earnings when the balloon pops.
elements.sent-2411-085: At any time, they are given the option to collect their earnings by clicking the ``collect the money'' button, before proceeding to the next balloon.

elements.sent-2411-149: The inter-beat interval (IBI) data were analyzed using the Kubios HRV standard software and further analysis using IBM SPSS V28.
elements.sent-2411-150: Parametric and nonparametric analyses of variance with corrected post hoc tests were used to evaluate the effect of the experimental conditions.
elements.sent-2411-151: EDA data were processed using the Ledalab toolbox within Matlab.
elements.sent-2411-152: The normality of the distributions is assessed using the Shapiro-Wilk test, with the significance level set at 0.05.
elements.sent-2411-153: Any identified outliers that were more than 5 standard deviations away from the mean were removed from the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-154: Additionally, to ensure the validity of the statistical inferences drawn from the data, the assumption of Sphericity was confirmed through the application of Mauchly's Test of Sphericity, which was conducted at a significance level of 0.05.
elements.sent-2411-155: The results of the Mauchly's W test and the corresponding p-values are reported in Table~\ref{tab4 .}
elements.sent-2411-156: For multiple comparisons, we conducted post hoc analyses using Tukey’s HSD test for between-subjects factors.
elements.sent-2411-157: For within-subject factors, we applied the Bonferroni correction to control for family-wise error rates due to multiple comparisons.
elements.sent-2411-158: The Bonferroni correction was chosen for within-subjects factors to account for potential violations of sphericity and to maintain a conservative control over Type I error across the correlated tests inherent in repeated measures \cite{lee2018proper, maxwell1980pairwise .}
elements.sent-2411-159: The Nao robot is a 58 cm tall and 5.6 kg programmable social humanoid, used in various application domains such as healthcare \cite{cespedes2021socially and education \cite{vogt2019second .}}
elements.sent-2411-160: While Nao is mostly used for non-tactile social interaction, it has been extended to allow affective tactile interaction to convey emotions to the robot \cite{andreasson2018affective .}
elements.sent-2411-161: However, in the studies reported here, we use a standard V5 Nao with no additional tactile sensory capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-162: The Multi-Dimensional Measure of Trust version 2 (MDMT v2) is a validated trust measurement tool designed to assess human-robot trust \cite{nomura2006measurement .}
elements.sent-2411-163: There are two broader factors of trust, consisting of five dimensions, which are Performance Trust (Reliable, Competent) and Moral Trust (Ethical, Transparent, Benevolent).
elements.sent-2411-164: The five dimensions consist of four items respectively, which are given below:.
elements.sent-2411-166: Reliable Subscale: reliable, predictable, dependable, consistent.
elements.sent-2411-167: Competent Subscale: competent, skilled, capable, meticulous.
elements.sent-2411-168: Ethical Subscale: ethical, principled, moral, has integrity.
elements.sent-2411-169: Transparent Subscale: transparent, genuine, sincere, candid.
elements.sent-2411-170: Benevolent Subscale: benevolent, kind, considerate, has goodwill.
elements.sent-2411-171: The Negative Attitude toward Robots Scale (NARS) is a measure of the negative attitudes and emotions of participants towards robots.
elements.sent-2411-172: It consists of three sub-scales ---negative attitudes toward situations of interaction with robots, negative attitudes toward the social influence of robots, and negative attitudes toward emotions in interaction with robots--- and has been widely used in HRI studies \cite{nomura2006altered .}
elements.sent-2411-173: The Empatica E4 sensor is a wearable device that is equipped with various sensors for collecting physiological data, including a photoplethysmography (PPG) sensor, an EDA sensor, a 3-axis accelerometer, and an infrared thermopile.
elements.sent-2411-174: The E4 sensor is unique in that it is the only wearable sensor that combines EDA and PPG sensors, allowing for the simultaneous measurement of sympathetic nervous system (SNS) activity and skin conductance.
elements.sent-2411-175: It has been used in earlier studies, for example, to measure EDA and Emotional Response by E4 sensor \cite{chan2020effect .}
elements.sent-2411-176: In the studies reported here, the E4 sensor was worn on the non-dominant hand of the participants.
elements.sent-2411-177: They were instructed to refrain from moving that hand to avoid any spurious readings.
elements.sent-2411-178: This procedure was implemented to ensure that the physiological data collected were a direct result of the experimental manipulation and not due to extraneous factors.
elements.sent-2411-179: The BART measures the propensity to take real-world risks.
elements.sent-2411-180: It is implemented as a game in which earnings are balanced against losses.
elements.sent-2411-181: In the task, participants inflate 30 different balloons by clicking a button.
elements.sent-2411-182: With each click, the balloon is inflated a little and 0.01 \euro\ is earned.
elements.sent-2411-183: Participants can choose to move on to the next balloon, at which time they collect the money earned for that balloon.
elements.sent-2411-184: If however the balloon is over-inflated and explodes, which happens after a pseudorandom number of pumps, the participant loses the money earned for that balloon.
elements.sent-2411-185: The larger the balloon, the greater the risk it will explode, but also the greater the potential reward.
elements.sent-2411-186: The BART results are complemented by a risk self-evaluation questionnaire.
elements.sent-2411-187: In the present study, various measures of performance on the BART were used to assess participants' risk-taking behaviour.
elements.sent-2411-188: The BART score, which is a commonly used measure in the literature, is calculated as the adjusted average number of pumps on unexploded balloons \cite{bornovalova2005differences,lejuez2002evaluation .}
elements.sent-2411-189: This score is a proxy for risk-taking behaviour, with higher scores indicating greater risk-taking propensity.
elements.sent-2411-190: In addition to the BART score, other standard measures of performance on the BART were also assessed, including (1) the total number of pumps by the participants, (2) the number of pumps at which a balloon explodes, (3) the number of exploded balloons, and (4) participants' profits for each balloon.
elements.sent-2411-191: These scores are the sum across the 30 trials.
elements.sent-2411-192: These measures have also been previously used in related literature \cite{hri2 .}
elements.subsubsection-2411-009: Gender difference in physiology and BART performance
elements.sent-2411-193: There is a large body of literature that describes gender differences in risk-taking behaviour, with the consensus being that males are more likely to take risks than females, and that females are less likely to engage in activities which are competitive and have less predictable outcomes \cite{fletschner2010women,lazanyi2017analysis .}
elements.sent-2411-194: Studies have also shown that men tend to engage in more risky behaviour than women across various domains \cite{harris2006gender, charness2012strong and that this gender difference is partially mediated by women paying more attention to potentially negative outcomes and experiencing lower satisfaction from risky tasks, and men are more aggressive, thrill-seeking and that they are willing to accept higher risks \cite{turner2003age , and that young boys more readily engage in risk-taking behaviour \cite{ginsburg1982sex .}}}
elements.sent-2411-195: One potential explanation for these gender differences is that young men are more likely to be encouraged by their peers to engage in risky behaviour \cite{davies2000identifying .}
elements.sent-2411-196: Additionally, societal expectations, cultural and environmental factors, and cognitive and emotional differences could also play a role in these gender differences in risk-taking behaviour \cite{byrnes1999gender,harris2006gender,fessler2004angry .}
elements.sent-2411-197: However, it is worth noting that this is an active area of research and the literature is not conclusive, with further research being needed to fully understand the underlying causes of gender differences in risk-taking behaviour.
elements.sent-2411-198: Based on this, we explore the gender differences in BART scores and HRV and the interaction between experimental conditions and gender.
elements.sent-2411-199: The robots' physical presence has been used to provide mental support in previous work \cite{shibata1999emergence .}
elements.sent-2411-200: In this context, arousal is often a key indicator and can be measured over longer intervals using physiological data, such as EDA and Heart Rate Variability (HRV) \cite{birenboim2019wearables .}
elements.sent-2411-201: For example, research has shown that remotely delivered touch can change the overall psychological affective experience as seen in the SCR \cite{chan2020effect .}
elements.sent-2411-202: In the present study, we measure arousal levels using EDA and HRV during the BART.
elements.sent-2411-203: Arousal is widely considered one of the two main aspects of emotional response, and while measuring arousal is not the same as measuring emotion, it is an important component of emotion \cite{caruelle2019use .}
elements.sent-2411-204: By measuring arousal, we aim to gain insights into how different intensities of tactile interaction affect emotional response and attention during the BART task.
elements.sent-2411-205: Heart rate variability (HRV) has been widely used as a measure to assess stress levels and emotional arousal, which measures the variations between heartbeats and is considered a proxy for stress \cite{thayer2012meta .}
elements.sent-2411-206: It can be analyzed using inter-beat interval (IBI) data recorded by the Empatica E4 sensor.
elements.sent-2411-207: However, stress levels can also be indirectly assessed through other measures, such as the Parasympathetic Nervous System (PNS), the sympathetic nervous system (SNS), and the Stress index.
elements.sent-2411-208: The Kubios HRV software can be used to perform HRV analysis by using IBI data, and it provides additional measures that are indicative of physiological elements connected to emotional regulation, such as the PNS index, the SNS index, and the stress (the square root of Baevsky's stress index) \cite{baevsky2008methodical .}
elements.sent-2411-209: It is well established that HRV increases in response to PNS activity (vagal stimulation) and decreases in response to SNS activity \cite{hri7,hri8,hri6 .}
elements.sent-2411-210: Stress also affects HRV, and current neurobiological research supports HRV as an objective indicator of psychological well-being and stress.
elements.sent-2411-211: When an individual is under stress or pressure, the high-frequency component of HRV is decreased \cite{Kim2018Stress .}
elements.sent-2411-212: Previous studies have found that HRV increases when people find continuous tactile stimulation pleasurable \cite{triscoli2017heart .}
elements.sent-2411-213: Lower PNS and higher SNS index values are associated with higher stress levels.
elements.sent-2411-214: In this study, participants' stress levels are measured during four different experimental conditions.
elements.sent-2411-215: Electrodermal Activity (EDA), also referred to as skin conductance, is a physiological response characterised by changes in the electrical conductance of the skin\cite{braithwaite2013guide .}
elements.sent-2411-216: These changes are triggered by external or internal stimuli and are thought to be indicative of emotional and physiological arousal \cite{nikula1991psychological .}
elements.sent-2411-217: EDA has been found to vary in relation to changes in attention, emotion, and motivational processes, making it a valuable tool for measuring an individual's emotional response.
elements.sent-2411-218: The use of EDA as an indicator of emotional response has been well-established in the literature.
elements.sent-2411-219: It is considered a valuable tool for understanding the physiological underpinnings of emotional responses, and for providing insights into the relationship between emotions, stress, and physiological responses \cite{figner2011using ,ventura2022new .}
elements.sent-2411-220: The use of EDA in the current study allows for the examination of the relationship between tactile interaction, emotional response, and physiological arousal in a controlled experimental setting.
elements.sent-2411-221: In this study, we use Ledalab, following \cite{chan2020effect , to examine the variations in significant skin responses triggered by tactile interaction compared to those without it.}
elements.sent-2411-222: Ledalab is used to analyse the EDA data collected from the Empatica E4 sensor.
elements.sent-2411-224: First, a low-pass filter was applied to minimize high-frequency noise.
elements.sent-2411-225: Subsequently, the Continuous Decomposition Analysis is employed to extract the signal properties of the underlying sudomotor nerve activity.
elements.sent-2411-226: By setting the threshold at 0.1, we identify significant peaks.
elements.sent-2411-227: To assess the correlation between touch occurrences and EDA peaks during low-intensity robot touch scenarios, we compare the timing of touch events with the timing of EDA peaks.
elements.sent-2411-228: The results revealed a 78\% coincidence, indicating that participants experience significant EDA peaks when they engage in tactile and emotion-laden interactions.
elements.sent-2411-229: Trust is essential to interaction at different levels, from one-on-one interaction to large-scale societal interactions \cite{nataliya2015trust .}
elements.sent-2411-230: During human-robot interaction, the outcome of an interaction is often influenced by trust \cite{billings2012human .}
elements.sent-2411-231: Earlier studies point out that human contact has a positive effect on the formation of trust.
elements.sent-2411-232: The opposite behaviour, deception and malfunctioning, is equally important.
elements.sent-2411-233: When robots deceive people or make mistakes, it reduces the trust in the robot \cite{walter2013trust .}
elements.sent-2411-234: We use the MDMT v2 questionnaire to measure trust in the robot.

elements.sent-2411-235: We administer a questionnaire to assess participants' perceptions of Nao and their attitudes towards the robot after the first interaction condition.
elements.sent-2411-236: The questionnaire consisted of 8 statements on Nao's perceived feelings and 6 statements on the participant's feelings, inspired by the previous research \cite{geva2020touching , using a 5-point Likert-scale (see the full questionnaire in Supplementary Materials).}

elements.subsubsection-2411-013: Relationship between trust, risk-taking behaviour and arousal
elements.sent-2411-238: The relationship between trust and risk is not clear yet, even though they have been linked in the literature \cite{siegrist2021trust .}
elements.sent-2411-239: Trust is divided into perceived trust (also known as subjective trust) and behavioural trust.
elements.sent-2411-240: Perceived trust is inversely related to perceived risk, and behavioural trust is related to risk-taking, The relationship between subjective trust and behavioural trust parallels the relationship between perceived risk and the risk-taking behaviours \cite{das2004risk .}
elements.sent-2411-241: Our data also allow us to study whether risk-taking behaviour is related to the extent to which humans trust robots -- an intriguing and as yet unexplored issue.

elements.sent-2411-283: To evaluate the EDA across the four conditions, we first normalised the EDA data by subtracting the baseline EDA for each participant to take individual skin conductance differences into consideration.
elements.sent-2411-284: Then, a Shapiro-Wilk test (with $\alpha$ = 0.05) was conducted to assess the data distribution, considering the limited sample size.
elements.sent-2411-285: The test did not provide evidence for a normal distribution of the data.
elements.sent-2411-286: Consequently, a non-parametric test known as Friedman's test (with $\alpha$ = 0.05) was used to compare the previously mentioned EDA data between the conditions.
elements.sent-2411-287: Which was found to be statistically significant and the effect size was measured using Kendall's W, was calculated to be 0.112 across the conditions, suggesting a modest level of agreement ($W(3,112) = 0.112, \chi^2(3) = 13.70, p=0.003$).
elements.sent-2411-288: The mean ranking for LI, HI, NT, and NR conditions is 3.03, 2.73, 2.11, and 2.14 respectively, as shown in Fig.~\ref{EDA_rank .}
elements.sent-2411-289: Further, pairwise comparisons with Bonferroni correction were executed to discern differences between the categories.
elements.sent-2411-290: Notably, the results show that the participants under the LI condition have significantly higher EDA than in NT ($p = 0.003)$ and NR ($p=0.002$) conditions.
elements.sent-2411-291: However, no significant differences were observed between the HI, NT, and NR conditions.

elements.sent-2411-382: The study has several constraints that could affect the generalisability of the results.

elements.sent-2411-431: Our study investigated the impact of tactile interaction with a social robot on risk-taking behaviour and stress levels.
elements.sent-2411-432: We show that both low-intensity and high-intensity tactile interaction encourages people to take more risks.

elements.sent-2411-434: We also observed a decrease in performance trust together with an increase in moral trust across all experimental conditions as the interaction unfolds.
elements.sent-2411-435: The intention and characteristics of the robot are important factors influencing trust levels, and future research should explore the influence of different robot characteristics on trust.
elements.sent-2411-436: The study also examined gender effects in performance and psychosocial measurements during the interaction, revealing that male participants showed a greater inclination towards risk-taking and displayed lower levels of stress compared to female participants, confirming the gender effects in risk-taking behaviour reported in the literature \cite{byrnes1999gender .}
elements.sent-2411-437: Notably, during low-intensity tactile interaction, female participants exhibited slightly lower stress levels than males.
elements.sent-2411-438: Under other conditions, female participants experienced higher stress levels.
elements.sent-2411-439: The study found that self-reported risk-taking behaviour correlated well with the risk they took in a task, as might be expected.
elements.sent-2411-440: Additionally, participants who held a more negative attitude towards robots took less risk, even when the robot encouraged them to take risks.
elements.sent-2411-441: However, when invited to touch the robot that would change and they would take more risk when encouraged to do so by the robot.
elements.sent-2411-442: This suggests that tactile interaction mitigated possible negative preconceptions about robots.
elements.sent-2411-443: This ties in with Block et al., who found that users experienced the robot as more natural, enjoyable, and intelligent after tactile interaction \cite{block2023arms .}
elements.sent-2411-444: Moreover, our study revealed a significant positive correlation between risk-taking and the participants' trust in the robot.
elements.sent-2411-445: This finding aligns with previous research indicating that initial impressions of a robot can influence human-robot trust in problem-solving scenarios \cite{xu2018impact .}
elements.sent-2411-446: Similarly, the study suggests that the initial impression of the robot impacts trust levels, which in turn influences risk-taking behaviour.
elements.sent-2411-447: An important result is that the strong affective response of people, as measured by their physiological response, is not caused by the physical action of touching the robot, but instead by people perceiving the robot as a social entity.
elements.sent-2411-448: Touching a non-social object, such as the plastic box in our studies, does not elicit an affective response.
elements.sent-2411-449: This shows that the robot is more than just an object to us, instead, the robot has a social presence and touching a robot has special significance.
elements.sent-2411-450: In conclusion, social robots have a role to play in promoting physical and emotional well-being and this can be enhanced by designing them to allow tactile engagement \cite{van2013touch .}
elements.sent-2411-451: The results of this study indicate that tactile interaction with a social robot can influence people's physiological responses and decisions and that these are modulated by people's attitudes towards the robot.
elements.sent-2411-452: Overall, the use of tactile interaction in Human-Robot Interaction has the potential to improve the physical and emotional well-being of individuals, particularly in the context of assistive and companion robots.
